title: India in Global Context: The Attention Economy Is Not Only an Indian Story
thesis: India's attention-extraction challenges are not unique; they are a mobile-first, price-sensitive variant of a global pattern, and India's scale gives its regulatory and design choices outsized influence.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: India ranks among the highest countries for daily smartphone screen time.

evidence.snippet: DataReportal's Digital 2025 Global Overview Report places India among the highest countries for average daily time spent on mobile devices.
The ITU Facts and Figures 2024 report notes that lower-middle-income countries, including India, have driven recent internet-user growth and show high mobile engagement.

counterevidence.summary: Screen-time rankings vary by survey methodology, device definition, and sample population; India's exact global rank is sensitive to these choices.

claims.claim: Brazil, Indonesia, and Nigeria show similar patterns of mobile-first entertainment and platform concentration.

evidence.snippet: ITU Facts and Figures 2024 highlights that emerging economies in Asia, Africa, and Latin America have grown fastest in internet access, with mobile as the primary mode and entertainment as a leading use case.
DataReportal's country-level data shows comparable daily mobile usage and social-media dominance in Brazil, Indonesia, Nigeria, and India.

counterevidence.summary: Each market has distinct language, income, and regulatory contexts; platform concentration is similar but not identical, and local competitors matter in some regions.

claims.claim: The US and China have larger domestic platform ecosystems but face similar debates about youth screen time and platform accountability.

evidence.snippet: The U.S. Surgeon General's 2023 advisory on social media and youth mental health warns that current evidence is sufficient to justify concern and action on platform design.
China's CAC provisions on algorithmic recommendations and minor-protection rules reflect government efforts to limit excessive youth screen time and platform power.
The EU Digital Services Act imposes due-diligence, transparency, and risk-management obligations on large platforms, reflecting a broader global turn toward platform accountability.

counterevidence.summary: The US and China differ sharply in legal tradition, enforcement capacity, and speech protections; their regulatory models are not directly transferable to India.

claims.claim: India's regulatory choices—DSA-style due diligence, app-store rules, data protection—will shape how the global South addresses extraction.

evidence.snippet: India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 establishes consent-based data processing and children's data protections, creating a baseline for platform design obligations.
The revised Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021 introduce due-diligence, grievance, and transparency obligations for intermediaries.
The EU Digital Services Act provides a reference model for due-diligence and risk-assessment regimes that India's rules partially adapt.

counterevidence.summary: India's regulatory capacity, judicial backlog, and industry influence may limit enforcement; other global South countries may follow different models, such as ASEAN or African Union frameworks.

sources.title: U.S. Surgeon General: Social Media and Youth Mental Health Advisory
China CAC: Provisions on the Management of Algorithmic Recommendations in Internet Information Services
India: Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023
India: Information Technology (Intermediary Guidelines and Digital Media Ethics Code) Rules, 2021

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit; each claim represents a distinct comparative or regulatory point.
Preserve the evidence-cautious, non-moralizing tone; India is a high-resolution case of a global pattern, not a uniquely failing one.
When citing figures, distinguish between DataReportal/ITU global estimates and India-specific measurements from elsewhere in the series.
Treat US and Chinese regulatory models as reference points, not templates; India's constitutional framework differs on speech and privacy.
Flag any source that is more than two years old or whose URL may have changed, and suggest a fresher replacement if available.
A global screen-time bar chart is embedded in the 'Scale Makes It Visible' section; cite DataReportal and ITU when discussing it.

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.
